abstract:
Query optimization is a critical task in database systems, focused on
determining the most efficient way to execute a query from an enormous set of
possible strategies. Traditional approaches rely on heuristic search methods
and cost predictions, but these often struggle with the complexity of the
search space and inaccuracies in performance estimation, leading to suboptimal
plan choices. This paper presents LLMOpt, a novel framework that leverages
Large Language Models (LLMs) to address these challenges through two innovative
components: (1) LLM for Plan Candidate Generation (LLMOpt(G)), which eliminates
heuristic search by utilizing the reasoning abilities of LLMs to directly
generate high-quality query plans, and (2) LLM for Plan Candidate Selection
(LLMOpt(S)), a list-wise cost model that compares candidates globally to
enhance selection accuracy. To adapt LLMs for query optimization, we propose
fine-tuning pre-trained models using optimization data collected offline.
Experimental results on the JOB, JOB-EXT, and Stack benchmarks show that
LLMOpt(G) and LLMOpt(S) outperform state-of-the-art methods, including
PostgreSQL, BAO, and HybridQO. Notably, LLMOpt(S) achieves the best practical
performance, striking a balance between plan quality and inference efficiency.

keyInsights: Fine-tuning pre-trained LLMs is critical for query optimization performance
List-wise plan selection balances accuracy and inference efficiency better than point-wise approaches
Distribution mismatch between generated and selected plans impacts combined component performance
originalProblem: Traditional query optimizers struggle with exponential search spaces and inaccurate cost models
Learning-based approaches face challenges with high end-to-end overhead, limiting real-world applicability
results: Achieved significant execution latency improvements over PostgreSQL and state-of-the-art baselines
LLMOpt(S) demonstrated lowest end-to-end latency while maintaining competitive plan quality
Generated plans showed lower mean and variance in execution time compared to heuristic methods
Plan selection accuracy exceeded traditional Tree-CNN and Tree-LSTM approaches
solution: LLMOpt(G) component directly generates high-quality query plan candidates using fine-tuned LLMs
LLMOpt(S) component performs list-wise plan selection to efficiently identify optimal execution strategies
Framework integrates with existing database systems through modified pg_hint_plan tool
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Renmin University introduce LLMOpt, a query optimization framework that leverages large language models to generate query plans and select optimal execution strategies, demonstrating reduced execution latency compared to traditional optimizers while maintaining practical end-to-end performance through a novel list-wise plan selection approach.

title: A Query Optimization Method Utilizing Large Language Models

abstract:
The growing adoption of large language models (LLMs) in business applications
has amplified interest in Natural Language to SQL (NL2SQL) solutions, in which
there is competing demand for high performance and efficiency. Domain- and
customer-specific requirements further complicate the problem. To address this
conundrum, we introduce Distill-C, a distilled customization framework tailored
for NL2SQL tasks. Distill-C utilizes large teacher LLMs to produce high-quality
synthetic data through a robust and scalable pipeline. Finetuning smaller and
open-source LLMs on this synthesized data enables them to rival or outperform
teacher models an order of magnitude larger. Evaluated on multiple challenging
benchmarks, Distill-C achieves an average improvement of 36% in execution
accuracy compared to the base models from three distinct LLM families.
Additionally, on three internal customer benchmarks, Distill-C demonstrates a
22.6% performance improvement over the base models. Our results demonstrate
that Distill-C is an effective, high-performing and generalizable approach for
deploying lightweight yet powerful NL2SQL models, delivering exceptional
accuracies while maintaining low computational cost.
title: Distill-C: Enhanced NL2SQL via Distilled Customization with LLMs

abstract: Text-to-SQL is a fundamental yet challenging task in the NLP area, aiming at translating natural language questions into SQL queries. While recent advances in large language models have greatly improved performance, most existing approaches depend on models with tens of billions of parameters or costly APIs, limiting their applicability in resource-constrained environments. For real world, especially on edge devices, it is crucial for Text-to-SQL to ensure cost-effectiveness. Therefore, enabling the light-weight models for Text-to-SQL is of great practical significance. However, smaller LLMs often struggle with complicated user instruction, redundant schema linking or syntax correctness. To address these challenges, we propose MCTS-SQL, a novel framework that uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to guide SQL generation through multi-step refinement. Since the light-weight models' weak performance of single-shot prediction, we generate better results through several trials with feedback. However, directly applying MCTS-based methods inevitably leads to significant time and computational overhead. Driven by this issue, we propose a token-level prefix-cache mechanism that stores prior information during iterations, effectively improved the execution speed. Experiments results on the SPIDER and BIRD benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Using a small open-source Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B, our method outperforms ChatGPT-3.5. When leveraging a more powerful model Gemini 2.5 to explore the performance upper bound, we achieved results competitive with the SOTA. Our findings demonstrate that even small models can be effectively deployed in practical Text-to-SQL systems with the right strategy.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunication

title: MCTS-SQL: Light-Weight LLMs can Master the Text-to-SQL through Monte Carlo Tree Search

abstract: Text-to-SQL, which enables natural language interaction with databases, serves as a pivotal method across diverse industries. With new, more powerful large language models (LLMs) emerging every few months, fine-tuning has become incredibly costly, labor-intensive, and error-prone. As an alternative, zero-shot Text-to-SQL, which leverages the growing knowledge and reasoning capabilities encoded in LLMs without task-specific fine-tuning, presents a promising and more challenging direction. To address this challenge, we propose Alpha-SQL, a novel approach that leverages a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework to iteratively infer SQL construction actions based on partial reasoning states. To enhance the framework's reasoning capabilities, we introduce LLM-as-Action-Model to dynamically generate SQL construction actions during the MCTS process, steering the search toward more promising SQL queries. Moreover, Alpha-SQL employs a self-supervised reward function to evaluate the quality of candidate SQL queries, ensuring more accurate and efficient query generation. Experimental results show that Alpha-SQL achieves 69.7% execution accuracy on the BIRD development set, using a 32B open-source LLM without fine-tuning. Alpha-SQL outperforms the best previous zero-shot approach based on GPT-4o by 2.5% on the BIRD development set.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Structured search algorithms like MCTS can significantly amplify the reasoning capabilities of LLMs for complex, multi-step generation tasks in a zero-shot context.
An LLM dynamically generating context-aware SQL construction actions and Chain-of-Thought reasoning within an MCTS framework leads to more deliberate and accurate query building.
Self-supervised reward functions, derived from execution consistency, offer an effective, label-free mechanism to guide search and correct LLM-generated outputs.
originalProblem: The escalating cost and labor required to fine-tune large language models (LLMs) for Text-to-SQL tasks with every new model release.
Existing zero-shot Text-to-SQL methods struggle to generalize LLM knowledge effectively for complex SQL generation, often leading to inaccuracies in schema alignment and query construction.
Challenges in robustly mapping natural language to SQL semantics across diverse database schemas without task-specific training.
results: Alpha-SQL achieved 69.7% execution accuracy on the BIRD development set using Qwen2.5-Coder-32B, exceeding the prior state-of-the-art zero-shot method (RSL-SQL using GPT-4o) by 2.5%.

Smaller open-source LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder-7B) integrated with Alpha-SQL demonstrated competitive or superior performance compared to larger, proprietary models like GPT-4o in zero-shot Text-to-SQL.
An ablation study showed the SQL Revision action (A6) contributed the most to performance, underscoring the importance of execution-guided self-correction in the framework.
solution: An MCTS-based framework is employed to treat SQL generation as a progressive search problem, decomposing complex queries into manageable sub-tasks.
An "LLM-as-Action-Model" dynamically generates SQL construction actions and Chain-of-Thought reasoning at each MCTS step, guided by seven human-inspired reasoning actions.
A self-supervised reward mechanism evaluates candidate SQL queries based on execution result consistency, eliminating the need for labeled reward data during the search process.
paper_summary.summary: A zero-shot Text-to-SQL framework, Alpha-SQL, combines Large Language Models with Monte Carlo Tree Search and a self-supervised reward function. This approach enables smaller, open-source LLMs to reach state-of-the-art execution accuracy, exceeding prior zero-shot methods and rivaling larger, proprietary models such as GPT-4o on benchmarks like BIRD and Spider.

title: Alpha-SQL: Zero-Shot Text-to-SQL using Monte Carlo Tree Search

abstract: Query optimization is a crucial problem in database systems that has been studied for decades. Learned query optimizers (LQOs) can improve performance over time by incorporating feedback; however, they suffer from cold-start issues and often require retraining when workloads shift or schemas change. Recent LLM-based query optimizers leverage pre-trained and fine-tuned LLMs to mitigate these challenges. Nevertheless, they neglect LLMs' in-context learning and execution records as feedback for continuous evolution. In this paper, we present SEFRQO, a Self-Evolving Fine-tuned RAG-based Query Optimizer. SEFRQO mitigates the cold-start problem of LQOs by continuously learning from execution feedback via a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework. We employ both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement fine-tuning to prepare the LLM to produce syntactically correct and performance-efficient query hints. Moreover, SEFRQO leverages the LLM's in-context learning capabilities by dynamically constructing prompts with references to similar queries and the historical execution record of the same query. This self-evolving paradigm iteratively optimizes the prompt to minimize query execution latency. Evaluations show that SEFRQO outperforms state-of-the-art LQOs, achieving up to 65.05% and 93.57% reductions in query latency on the CEB and Stack workloads, respectively, compared to PostgreSQL.
title: SEFRQO: A Self-Evolving Fine-Tuned RAG-Based Query Optimizer

abstract:
Recent text-to-SQL systems powered by large language models (LLMs) have
demonstrated remarkable performance in translating natural language queries
into SQL. However, these systems often struggle with complex database
structures and domain-specific queries, as they primarily focus on enhancing
logical reasoning and SQL syntax while overlooking the critical need for
comprehensive database understanding. To address this limitation, we propose
DB-Explore, a novel framework that systematically aligns LLMs with database
knowledge through automated exploration and instruction synthesis. DB-Explore
constructs database graphs to capture complex relational schemas, leverages
GPT-4 to systematically mine structural patterns and semantic knowledge, and
synthesizes instructions to distill this knowledge for efficient fine-tuning of
LLMs. Our framework enables comprehensive database understanding through
diverse sampling strategies and automated instruction generation, bridging the
gap between database structures and language models. Experiments conducted on
the SPIDER and BIRD benchmarks validate the effectiveness of DB-Explore,
achieving an execution accuracy of 67.0% on BIRD and 87.8% on SPIDER. Notably,
our open-source implementation based on Qwen2.5-Coder-7B achieves
state-of-the-art results at minimal computational cost, outperforming several
GPT-4-driven Text-to-SQL systems.
title: DB-Explore: Automated Database Exploration and Instruction Synthesis for  Text-to-SQL

abstract: Recent advances in LLM-based Text-to-SQL have achieved remarkable gains on public benchmarks such as BIRD and Spider. Yet, these systems struggle to scale in realistic enterprise settings with large, complex schemas, diverse SQL dialects, and expensive multi-step reasoning. Emerging agentic approaches show potential for adaptive reasoning but often suffer from inefficiency and instability-repeating interactions with databases, producing inconsistent outputs, and occasionally failing to generate valid answers. To address these challenges, we introduce Agent Semantic Memory (AgentSM), an agentic framework for Text-to-SQL that builds and leverages interpretable semantic memory. Instead of relying on raw scratchpads or vector retrieval, AgentSM captures prior execution traces-or synthesizes curated ones-as structured programs that directly guide future reasoning. This design enables systematic reuse of reasoning paths, which allows agents to scale to larger schemas, more complex questions, and longer trajectories efficiently and reliably. Compared to state-of-the-art systems, AgentSM achieves higher efficiency by reducing average token usage and trajectory length by 25% and 35%, respectively, on the Spider 2.0 benchmark. It also improves execution accuracy, reaching a state-of-the-art accuracy of 44.8% on the Spider 2.0 Lite benchmark.

keyInsights: Systematic reuse of structured execution trajectories, rather than raw context, substantially improves agent efficiency and accuracy by eliminating redundant data exploration.
Composite tools are crucial for streamlining complex multi-step processes, reducing unnecessary tool usage, and enhancing the consistency and robustness of agent actions.
A dedicated Schema Linking Agent prevents the main Planner Agent from drifting during complex schema exploration, maintaining focus on core query generation and refinement tasks.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based Text-to-SQL systems struggle with large, complex enterprise schemas, diverse SQL dialects, and multi-step reasoning.
Agentic Text-to-SQL systems suffer from inefficiency due to repeated data exploration for similar queries, leading to high token usage and latency.
These systems also exhibit instability and high variance in outcomes, as minor errors can derail complex iterative reasoning processes.
results: AgentSM achieved a state-of-the-art execution accuracy of 44.8% on the Spider 2.0 Lite benchmark using Claude 4 Sonnet, a 14.1% improvement over the prior agentic baseline.

The method reduced average agent trajectory length by 25% and improved execution accuracy by 35% through the use of trajectory reading, leading to significant gains in efficiency, token usage, and latency.

Ablation studies confirmed that both trajectory reading and composite tools are critical, with their removal resulting in substantial decreases in accuracy (34.7% and 35.8% respectively) and increased average steps per trajectory.
solution: AgentSM introduces a streamlined two-agent architecture consisting of a Planner Agent for high-level reasoning and a specialized Schema Linking Agent for fine-grained data exploration.
It implements a novel structured semantic memory that captures and reuses entire execution trajectories (past reasoning steps and tool usages) from synthetic questions to guide new queries.

The framework incorporates 'composite tools' by identifying and combining frequently co-occurring sequences of single-purpose tools into higher-level operations, reducing steps and improving efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: The AgentSM framework enhances agentic Text-to-SQL systems by employing a structured semantic memory and composite tools. This approach achieves a state-of-the-art execution accuracy of 44.8% on the Spider 2.0 Lite benchmark, while reducing average trajectory length by 25% and improving efficiency.

title: AgentSM: Semantic Memory for Agentic Text-to-SQL

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been widely adopted to enhance the performance of large language models (LLMs) on Text-to-SQL tasks. However, existing methods often rely on execution-based or LLM-based Bradley-Terry reward models. The former suffers from high execution latency caused by repeated database calls, whereas the latter imposes substantial GPU memory overhead, both of which significantly hinder the efficiency and scalability of RL pipelines. To this end, we propose a novel reward model framework for RL-based Text-to-SQL named Graph-Reward-SQL, which employs the GMNScore outcome reward model. We leverage SQL graph representations to provide accurate reward signals while significantly reducing time cost and GPU memory usage. Building on this foundation, we further introduce StepRTM, a stepwise reward model that provides intermediate supervision over Common Table Expression (CTE) subqueries. This encourages both functional correctness and readability of SQL. Extensive comparative and ablation experiments on standard benchmarks, including Spider and BIRD, demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing reward models.

keyInsights: Relational Operator Trees (ROTs) offer a more robust semantic representation of SQL than Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs), improving the accuracy of functional equivalence assessment.

Graph Matching Networks can accurately assess deep semantic equivalence between SQL queries without requiring database execution, providing an efficient and accurate alternative to traditional reward models.

Providing fine-grained, stepwise rewards for intermediate query structures like CTEs significantly enhances the learning process for complex SQL generation, leading to improved accuracy and readability.
originalProblem: Execution-based reward models for Text-to-SQL RL incur high latency and computational costs due to repeated database interactions.
LLM-based reward models are computationally intensive and memory-heavy, limiting their scalability for fine-tuning large policy models.
Existing syntactic matching-based rewards are often superficial, failing to capture deep semantic equivalence and leading to inaccurate feedback signals.
Sparse, holistic reward signals hinder learning for complex SQL queries, especially those involving multiple logical steps like Common Table Expressions (CTEs).
results: Graph-Reward-SQL achieved competitive Execution Accuracy (e.g., 81.62% on Spider and 63.04% on BIRD) with a Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Ins model, performing comparably to or better than complex multi-agent systems.

GMNScore demonstrated high efficiency with an average per-sample reward-scoring latency of 0.023s (compared to 1.088s for execution-based rewards) and low GPU memory usage (83MB vs. 9304MB for LLM-based BTRM).
Integrating StepRTM improved Test-Suite accuracy by up to 5.87% on the BIRD dataset, indicating the benefit of stepwise feedback for handling complex and challenging queries.
solution: A GMNScore (Graph Matching Network Score) acts as an execution-free outcome reward, using Relational Operator Trees (ROTs) and a Graph Matching Network to assess functional equivalence between generated and reference SQL queries.
StepRTM (Stepwise Reward for Tree Matching) provides intermediate supervision by evaluating the generation of CTE subqueries, promoting structured and functionally correct query construction.
These reward models are integrated into a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) framework to fine-tune Large Language Models, combining robust outcome rewards with fine-grained intermediate feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Graph-Reward-SQL is an execution-free reinforcement learning framework for Text-to-SQL, which utilizes graph matching for outcome rewards and stepwise rewards for intermediate feedback. This approach substantially reduces training latency and GPU memory consumption while enhancing the accuracy and semantic correctness of generated SQL queries.

title: Graph-Reward-SQL: Execution-Free Reinforcement Learning for Text-to-SQL via Graph Matching and Stepwise Reward

abstract: Query rewriting is an effective technique for refining poorly written queries before they reach the query optimizer. However, manual rewriting is not scalable, as it is prone to errors and requires deep expertise. Traditional query rewriting algorithms fall short too: rule-based approaches fail to generalize to new query patterns, while synthesis-based methods struggle with complex queries. Fortunately, Large Language Models (LLMs) already possess broad knowledge and advanced reasoning capabilities, making them a promising solution for tackling these longstanding challenges. In this paper, we present GenRewrite, the first holistic system that leverages LLMs for query rewriting beyond traditional rules. We introduce the notion of Natural Language Rewrite Rules (NLR2s), which serve as hints for the LLM while also a means of knowledge transfer from rewriting one query to another, allowing GenRewrite to become smarter and more effective over time. We present a novel counterexample-guided technique that iteratively corrects the syntactic and semantic errors in the rewritten query, significantly reducing the LLM costs and the manual effort required for verification. Across the standard TPC-DS and JOB benchmarks and their SQLStorm-generated variants, GenRewrite consistently optimizes more queries at every speedup threshold than all baselines. At the >=2x threshold on TPC-DS, GenRewrite improves 25 queries-1.35x more than LLM-driven baselines and 2.6x more than LLM-enhanced rule-based baselines-and the gap widens further on TPC-DS (SQLStorm); on JOB and its SQLStorm variant, where queries are simpler, absolute gains are smaller but GenRewrite still leads by a notable margin.
title: GenRewrite: Query Rewriting via Large Language Models

abstract: Integrating LLM powered operators in declarative query languages allows for the combination of cheap and interpretable functions with powerful, generalizable language model reasoning. However, in order to benefit from the optimized execution of a database query language like SQL, generated outputs must align with the rules enforced by both type checkers and database contents. Current approaches address this challenge with orchestrations consisting of many LLM-based post-processing calls to ensure alignment between generated outputs and database values, introducing performance bottlenecks. We perform a study on the ability of various sized open-source language models to both parse and execute functions within a query language based on SQL, showing that small language models can excel as function executors over hybrid data sources. Then, we propose an efficient solution to enforce the well-typedness of LLM functions, demonstrating 7% accuracy improvement on a multi-hop question answering dataset with 53% improvement in latency over comparable solutions. We make our implementation available at this https URL
title: Play by the Type Rules: Inferring Constraints for LLM Functions in Declarative Programs

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in translating natural language questions into SQL queries (Text-to-SQL). In contrast, small language models (SLMs) ranging from 0.5B to 1.5B parameters currently underperform on Text-to-SQL tasks due to their limited logical reasoning capabilities. However, SLMs offer inherent advantages in inference speed and suitability for edge deployment. To explore their potential in Text-to-SQL applications, we leverage recent advancements in post-training techniques. Specifically, we used the open-source SynSQL-2.5M dataset to construct two derived datasets: SynSQL-Think-916K for SQL generation and SynSQL-Merge-Think-310K for SQL merge revision. We then applied supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning-based post-training to the SLM, followed by inference using a corrective self-consistency approach. Experimental results validate the effectiveness and generalizability of our method, SLM-SQL. On the BIRD development set, the five evaluated models achieved an average improvement of 31.4 points. Notably, the 0.5B model reached 56.87\% execution accuracy (EX), while the 1.5B model achieved 67.08\% EX. We will release our dataset, model, and code to github: this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Small Language Models (0.5B-1.5B parameters) can achieve competitive Text-to-SQL execution accuracy, often outperforming larger models, when specifically trained with advanced post-training techniques.

A multi-faceted training approach combining synthetic data-driven Supervised Fine-Tuning for Chain-of-Thought, Reinforcement Learning with execution-based rewards, and a corrective self-consistency mechanism is crucial for enhancing SLM reasoning capabilities.

Each component (SFT, RL, CSC) of the SLM-SQL framework independently contributes to performance improvements, with SFT being particularly critical for initial instruction following and CSC for error correction during inference.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in Text-to-SQL but are computationally expensive for inference and resource-intensive, hindering deployment in constrained environments.
Small Language Models (SLMs) offer faster inference and suitability for edge deployment, but typically underperform LLMs in complex Text-to-SQL tasks due to perceived limited logical reasoning.
Existing state-of-the-art Text-to-SQL approaches predominantly rely on LLMs, leaving the capabilities of SLMs (0.5B-1.5B parameters) largely underexplored for this challenging task.
results: SLM-SQL significantly boosted Text-to-SQL execution accuracy (EX) for small language models (SLMs), achieving an average 31.4 percentage point improvement on BIRD. The 1.5B SLM-SQL model reached 67.08% EX on BIRD, outperforming multiple larger open-source (7B-32B) and some closed-source LLM-based methods.

The framework showed robust generalization to the Spider dataset, with models trained only on BIRD achieving an average 24.98 percentage point EX improvement on the test set, and the 1.5B model reaching 79.06% EX without dataset-specific RL training.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical importance of each component: Supervised Fine-Tuning (up to 21.93 point drop), Corrective Self-Consistency (approx. 5 point drop), and Reinforcement Learning (approx. 4-5 point drop) to the overall performance.
solution: Developed SLM-SQL, a multi-stage framework specifically designed for Small Language Models (SLMs) that integrates synthetic data construction, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning (RL), and a corrective self-consistency (CSC) inference mechanism.
Constructed two specialized synthetic datasets, SynSQL-Think-916K and SynSQL-Merge-Think-310K, to enable SLMs to generate logical reasoning (Chain-of-Thought) and perform SQL merge revisions.

Employed a two-stage training approach: initial SFT on synthetic data for CoT generation, followed by RL using Group Relative Policy Optimization with execution-based rewards to refine SQL accuracy and formatting.
paper_summary.summary: A framework from Wuhan University of Technology and the University of Science and Technology of China enables small language models (0.5B-1.5B parameters) to achieve competitive Text-to-SQL performance by adapting advanced post-training techniques. The SLM-SQL method improved execution accuracy by an average of 31.4 percentage points on the BIRD dataset, with its 1.5B model reaching 67.08% EX and surpassing several larger models.

title: SLM-SQL: An Exploration of Small Language Models for Text-to-SQL

abstract:
Text-to-SQL (Text2SQL) aims to map natural language questions to executable
SQL queries. Although large language models (LLMs) have driven significant
progress, current approaches struggle with poor transferability to open-source
LLMs, limited robustness against logic and function errors in complex queries,
and inefficiencies in structured search. We introduce SQL-o1, a
self-reward-driven heuristic search framework built on an agent-based
architecture to enhance model reasoning capabilities. SQL-o1 leverages Monte
Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for structured, multi-step exploration, and
incorporates a dynamic pruning strategy to accelerate inference without
sacrificing accuracy. On the Spider and Bird benchmarks, SQL-o1 achieves a
+10.8 execution accuracy improvement on the complex Bird dataset, surpassing
even GPT-4-based models. Notably, it exhibits strong few-shot generalization
and robust cross-model transferability across open-source LLMs. Our code is
available at:this https URL

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: SQL-o1: A Self-Reward Heuristic Dynamic Search Method for Text-to-SQL

abstract: Text-to-SQL, the process of translating natural language into Structured Query Language (SQL), represents a transformative application of large language models (LLMs), potentially revolutionizing how humans interact with data. This paper introduces the SQL-PaLM framework, a comprehensive solution for understanding and enhancing Text-to-SQL using LLMs, using in the learning regimes of few-shot prompting and instruction fine-tuning. With few-shot prompting, we explore the effectiveness of consistency decoding with execution-based error filtering. With instruction fine-tuning, we delve deep in understanding the critical paradigms that influence the performance of tuned LLMs. In particular, we investigate how performance can be improved through expanded training data coverage and diversity, synthetic data augmentation, and integrating query-specific database content. We propose a test-time selection method to further refine accuracy by integrating SQL outputs from multiple paradigms with execution feedback as guidance. Additionally, we tackle the practical challenge of navigating intricate databases with a significant number of tables and columns, proposing efficient techniques for accurately selecting relevant database elements to enhance Text-to-SQL performance. Our holistic approach yields substantial advancements in Text-to-SQL, as demonstrated on two key public benchmarks, Spider and BIRD. Through comprehensive ablations and error analyses, we shed light on the strengths and weaknesses of our framework, offering valuable insights into Text-to-SQL's future work.

keyInsights: Fine-tuning large language models provides substantial performance gains over few-shot prompting for Text-to-SQL, especially on complex datasets, highlighting the benefit of direct parameter updates.
Enhancing training data through diversity (mixing datasets with complex SQLs) and augmentation (LLM-generated synthetic queries) improves model generalization and performance.
Integrating query-specific database content and utilizing execution-based feedback loops are crucial for improving the accuracy and robustness of SQL generation.
originalProblem: Accurately converting natural language questions into SQL queries, especially for complex and ambiguous questions.
Handling real-world database challenges such as vast and intricate schemas, diverse data types, and the high cost of collecting labeled training data.
Overcoming the limitations of few-shot prompting in large language models, which often struggles with the semantic and structural complexities of real-world Text-to-SQL scenarios.
results: SQL-PaLM achieved state-of-the-art results, with 61.7% Execution Accuracy on the BIRD development set and 87.3% EX / 83.5% Test-Suite Accuracy on the Spider development set.
Instruction fine-tuning demonstrated superior performance over few-shot prompting, showing an 8.51% gain in EX on BIRD, and larger PaLM-2 models consistently yielded better results.

Incorporating query-specific database content improved BIRD performance by 3.65% EX, while diverse training data boosted Spider EX by 4%, and program-aided column selection effectively managed large schemas with 92.60% precision.
solution: A comprehensive framework, SQL-PaLM, which leverages instruction fine-tuning of PaLM-2 models, using a meticulously designed input representation for database schemas and natural language questions.

Systematic investigation into critical tuning paradigms including diversifying training data coverage by combining multiple datasets, augmenting with LLM-generated synthetic SQL queries, and integrating query-specific database content.

Development of efficient table and column selection techniques (retrieval-based and program-aided) to manage large database schemas, alongside a test-time refinement method that uses execution-based selection to combine outputs from multiple paradigms.
paper_summary.summary: SQL-PaLM, developed by Google's Cloud AI Research Team and Google DeepMind, presents a comprehensive framework for fine-tuning large language models, specifically PaLM-2, to convert natural language into SQL queries. The framework integrates diverse data sources, synthetic data augmentation, and intelligent column selection, achieving state-of-the-art results on the BIRD and Spider Text-to-SQL benchmarks.

title: SQL-PaLM: Improved Large Language Model Adaptation for Text-to-SQL (extended)

abstract: Translating natural language into SQL (Text-to-SQL) remains a core challenge at the intersection of language understanding and structured data access. Although large language models (LLMs) have improved fluency, generating correct and executable SQL, especially for complex queries, continues to be challenging. We introduce CogniSQL-R1-Zero, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework and model that produces accurate SQL using a lightweight reward signal based on execution correctness and format-tag compliance. By avoiding intermediate supervision, hybrid pipelines and complex reward shaping, our method encourages stable learning and stronger alignment with the ultimate task objective-producing executable programs. CogniSQL-R1-Zero achieves state-of-the-art execution accuracy on Text2SQL benchmark; BIRD bench, outperforming prior supervised and instruction-tuned baselines including SFT CodeS-7B, DeepSeek-Coder 236B, and Mistral 123B-despite being trained on a significantly smaller 7B backbone. This result underscores the scalability and efficiency of our RL-based approach when trained on just four NVIDIA A100 GPUs (40 GB VRAM each). To support further research in efficient and interpretable Text-to-SQL modeling, we release two curated datasets: (i) a collection of 5,024 reasoning traces with varying context lengths, and (ii) a positive-sampled corpus of 36,356 corpus of weakly supervised queries, each annotated with six semantically diverse reasoning paths. Together, these contributions advance scalable, execution-aligned Text-to-SQL generation.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art Text-to-SQL performance can be achieved with a relatively lightweight 7B-parameter model when strategically trained with execution-aligned reinforcement learning.
Sparse, execution-based reward signals are sufficient for effectively refining LLM capabilities in complex code generation tasks, minimizing the need for dense intermediate supervision.
Test-time scaling, such as best-of-N sampling (e.g., best-of-6), offers substantial performance improvements for Text-to-SQL with minimal additional overhead.
originalProblem: Text-to-SQL generation historically struggled with generalization and semantic correctness, even when queries were syntactically valid.
Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently produce plausible-looking but functionally incorrect SQL, leading to a persistent "fluency-execution gap" on complex queries.

State-of-the-art Text-to-SQL solutions, often involving large models or complex hybrid pipelines, incur high computational costs and inference latency, making them impractical for broad deployment under low-compute constraints.
results: Achieved 59.97% execution accuracy (Ex%) on the BIRD development set with single-sample generation, outperforming GPT-4 (46.35%) and DeepSeek-Coder 236B (56.13%).
Demonstrated an absolute +7.95% gain in execution accuracy, improving from the base model's 52.02% to 59.97% after reinforcement learning optimization.
Showcased efficient training on four NVIDIA A100 GPUs (40 GB VRAM each) with DeepSpeed ZeRO 2 and gradient accumulation, yielding a ~2.96x speedup compared to single-GPU training.
solution: A lightweight reinforcement learning (RL) framework was developed using a Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct backbone with Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT).
Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) was employed, optimizing directly for SQL execution correctness via a multi-component reward function without a separate value network.
Low-compute training optimizations, including DeepSpeed ZeRO 2 and gradient accumulation, enabled efficient training on four NVIDIA A100 GPUs.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Dell Technologies and UT Austin introduced CogniSQL-R1-Zero, a lightweight reinforced reasoning approach for Text-to-SQL generation. This 7B-parameter model achieved 59.97% execution accuracy on the BIRD development set, surpassing larger LLMs like GPT-4 and DeepSeek-Coder 236B, while training efficiently on four NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

title: CogniSQL-R1-Zero: Lightweight Reinforced Reasoning for Efficient SQL Generation

abstract: Text2SQL, the task of generating SQL queries from natural language text, is a critical challenge in data engineering. Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated superior performance for this task due to their advanced comprehension and generation capabilities. However, privacy and cost considerations prevent companies from using Text2SQL solutions based on external LLMs offered as a service. Rather, small LLMs (SLMs) that are openly available and can hosted in-house are adopted. These SLMs, in turn, lack the generalization capabilities of larger LLMs, which impairs their effectiveness for complex tasks such as Text2SQL. To address these limitations, we propose MATS, a novel Text2SQL framework designed specifically for SLMs. MATS uses a multi-agent mechanism that assigns specialized roles to auxiliary agents, reducing individual workloads and fostering interaction. A training scheme based on reinforcement learning aligns these agents using feedback obtained during execution, thereby maintaining competitive performance despite a limited LLM size. Evaluation results using on benchmark datasets show that MATS, deployed on a single- GPU server, yields accuracy that are on-par with large-scale LLMs when using significantly fewer parameters. Our source code and data are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decomposing complex Text2SQL tasks into specialized sub-tasks allows SLMs to overcome their individual reasoning limitations and collectively achieve high performance.
Integrating database execution feedback through RLEF provides an effective and scalable mechanism for agent alignment, enabling SLMs to learn correct SQL generation without explicit human labels.

The modified ORPO algorithm proves superior for SLM alignment, effectively balancing supervised fine-tuning gains with preference learning while consuming less memory and training time compared to PPO and DPO.
originalProblem: High-performing Text2SQL solutions rely on large, proprietary LLMs, raising privacy concerns and incurring substantial API costs.
Standalone open-source LLMs require significant computational resources and often deliver lower accuracy for complex Text2SQL tasks.
Small Language Models (SLMs) struggle with deep reasoning, complex context understanding, and instruction following in Text2SQL due to their inherent limitations.
results: MATS achieved 64.73% execution accuracy (EX%) on the BIRD dev set and 87.1% EX% on the Spider dev set, performing on par with or surpassing much larger open-source and proprietary LLMs.
The framework requires only 21.2 GB of GPU memory, allowing deployment on a single 24GB GPU server, significantly reducing resource demands compared to CodeS-15B (36GB).
RLEF consistently improved the Planner Agent's EX% (e.g., from 53.65% to 59.32% on BIRD) and the overall MATS framework's accuracy, demonstrating the critical role of execution feedback.
solution: The MATS framework decomposes the Text2SQL task into five specialized SLM agents: Schema Insight, Planner, Validator, Fix, and Selection, each handling specific sub-tasks.
Supervised fine-tuning of SLMs uses a data generation approach involving manual few-shot examples and GPT-4o-mini to create structured reasoning prompt-response pairs.

Reinforcement Learning from Execution Feedback (RLEF) aligns agent policies using database execution results as feedback, employing an optimized ORPO algorithm for efficient, reward-model-free training.
paper_summary.summary: MATS, a multi-agent framework, leverages small language models (SLMs) and execution feedback to translate natural language into SQL queries, achieving execution accuracy comparable to large language models while operating on significantly fewer computational resources (e.g., 21.2 GB GPU memory). This approach enhances data privacy and reduces costs by enabling in-house deployment.

title: A Multi-agent Text2SQL Framework using Small Language Models and Execution Feedback

abstract: The data-centric paradigm has emerged as a pivotal direction in artificial intelligence (AI), emphasizing the role of high-quality training data. This shift is especially critical in the Text-to-SQL task, where the scarcity, limited diversity, and structural simplicity of existing datasets constrain model performance. To address these challenges, we propose Text2SQL-Flow, a SQL-aware data augmentation framework that systematically generates large-scale, semantically valid, and structurally diverse Text-to-SQL pairs from limited seed data. Our framework spans six augmentation dimensions and integrates an end-to-end pipeline with auxiliary database selection, SQL executability verification, natural language (NL) question generation, NL-SQL correspondence verification, and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning trace generation. Leveraging this framework, we construct SQLFlow, a high-quality dataset comprising 75,386 annotated examples. We demonstrate the utility of SQLFlow in both fine-tuning and prompt-based settings. (1) For open-source large language models (LLMs), fine-tuning with SQLFlow improves problem-solving ability, delivering competitive gains across multiple benchmarks under the same data budget. (2) For closed-source LLMs, we propose a masked alignment retrieval method that uses SQLFlow as both a knowledge base and training data for the retrieval model, enabling structure-aware example matching via fine-grained NL-SQL alignments. Experiments show that our retrieval strategy outperforms existing example retrieval methods, highlighting the combined value of SQLFlow's data quality and our retrieval technique. Overall, our work provides a scalable, data-centric foundation for advancing Text-to-SQL systems and underscores the importance of structured, high-fidelity data in modern AI development. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Systematic and quality-controlled data augmentation, rather than mere quantity, is critical for advancing Text-to-SQL performance with LLMs, reinforcing the data-centric AI paradigm.
Combining multi-dimensional augmentation strategies with rigorous filtering and Chain-of-Thought supervision effectively creates diverse, high-quality data crucial for model reasoning.

A masked alignment retrieval approach that emphasizes structural correspondence between natural language and SQL queries significantly outperforms traditional semantic similarity for few-shot Text-to-SQL learning.
originalProblem: Text-to-SQL model development is constrained by the scarcity, limited diversity, and structural simplicity of high-quality training data.
Manually annotated Text-to-SQL datasets are expensive to create, limited in scale, and often lack comprehensive coverage of complex SQL patterns.
The full potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) in Text-to-SQL cannot be realized without richer and more varied datasets for fine-tuning or in-context learning.
results: The SQLFLOW dataset (75,386 examples) exhibits substantially increased query complexity and diversity, with augmented data showing a marked rise in advanced SQL features compared to original seed data.

Fine-tuning open-source LLMs on SQLFLOW leads to significant performance gains, achieving 83.4% execution accuracy on Spider-dev and 53.7% on EHRSQL with Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, surpassing baselines and proving competitive with much larger synthetic datasets.

The masked alignment retrieval method dramatically improves few-shot performance for closed-source LLMs, outperforming other retrieval strategies for models like GPT-4o by emphasizing structural similarity.
solution: TEXT2SQL-FLOW, an end-to-end SQL-aware framework, augments data by introducing auxiliary databases for distribution-level diversity and applying six SQL augmentation strategies for query-level complexity.
Rigorous multi-stage filtering (SQL executability, NL-SQL correspondence) ensures the semantic validity and quality of the generated natural language and SQL pairs.

A novel masked alignment retrieval method is proposed for closed-source LLMs, training an embedding model on masked (NL question, SQL query) pairs to improve few-shot example selection based on structural similarity.
paper_summary.summary: A SQL-aware data augmentation framework, TEXT2SQL-FLOW, constructs the SQLFLOW dataset of 75,386 diverse Text-to-SQL pairs, substantially boosting performance for open-source LLMs through fine-tuning and for closed-source LLMs via a masked alignment retrieval method. Models fine-tuned on SQLFLOW achieved up to 83.4% execution accuracy on Spider-dev and 53.7% on EHRSQL, outperforming baselines and models trained on other synthetic datasets.

title: Text2SQL-Flow: A Robust SQL-Aware Data Augmentation Framework for Text-to-SQL

abstract:
Current self-correction approaches in text-to-SQL face two critical
limitations: 1) Conventional self-correction methods rely on recursive
self-calls of LLMs, resulting in multiplicative computational overhead, and 2)
LLMs struggle to implement effective error detection and correction for
declarative SQL queries, as they fail to demonstrate the underlying reasoning
path. In this work, we propose SHARE, an SLM-based Hierarchical Action
corREction assistant that enables LLMs to perform more precise error
localization and efficient correction. SHARE orchestrates three specialized
Small Language Models (SLMs) in a sequential pipeline, where it first
transforms declarative SQL queries into stepwise action trajectories that
reveal underlying reasoning, followed by a two-phase granular refinement. We
further propose a novel hierarchical self-evolution strategy for data-efficient
training. Experimental results demonstrate that SHARE effectively enhances
self-correction capabilities while proving robust across various LLMs.
Furthermore, our comprehensive analysis shows that SHARE maintains strong
performance even in low-resource training settings, which is particularly
valuable for text-to-SQL applications with data privacy constraints.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: SHARE introduces the first SLM-LLM collaborative framework for text-to-SQL error correction, where specialized small language models guide larger generator LLMs.

It transforms opaque SQL queries into transparent, step-by-step "action trajectories" (pandas-like API calls) that explicitly capture the logical flow, enabling more precise and granular error correction.

A hierarchical self-evolution strategy is employed for data augmentation, significantly reducing the reliance on expensive teacher LLM queries and annotation costs by synthesizing training data for subsequent models.
originalProblem: Existing self-debugging approaches for text-to-SQL rely on external database execution, leading to insufficient feedback specificity, operational constraints, and privacy/security concerns.

Current self-correction methods suffer from prohibitive computational costs due to multiple expensive LLM inferences and exhibit self-enhancement bias where LLMs overestimate their initial output quality.
Debugging declarative SQL is challenging for LLMs due to the lack of explicit reasoning paths within the query structure, making error identification difficult.
results: SHARE-8B, when paired with GPT-4o, achieved a 14.80% relative improvement on the BIRD dataset and 11.41% on SPIDER, and up to 28.64% improvement when used with Claude-3.5-Sonnet.

The framework demonstrated robust generalization, showing effective performance on MySQL and PostgreSQL dialects without additional training, and consistently improved results across various query complexities.

SHARE reduced inference costs to one-tenth of baseline methods and significantly lowered training data construction costs, while effectively mitigating both schema-based and logic-based errors with lower overcorrection rates.
solution: The SHARE framework employs a sequential pipeline orchestrating three specialized Small Language Models (SLMs): a Base Action Model (BAM), a Schema Augmentation Model (SAM), and a Logic Optimization Model (LOM).

It converts initial SQL queries into detailed action trajectories, which are then refined by SAM for schema-linking errors and by LOM for logical correctness using action-based perturbation strategies.
The SLMs are fine-tuned using LoRA adaptation, and a hierarchical self-evolution strategy is used to synthesize high-quality training data, reducing dependency on expensive teacher LLM supervision.
paper_summary.summary: The SHARE framework introduces a method for text-to-SQL error correction by using specialized Small Language Models (SLMs) to guide larger generator LLMs. It transforms SQL queries into interpretable 'action trajectories,' enabling precise and cost-effective debugging, and achieves up to 14.80% relative improvement on the BIRD dataset while reducing inference cost to one-tenth of baseline methods.

title: SHARE: An SLM-based Hierarchical Action CorREction Assistant for  Text-to-SQL

abstract:
When complex SQL queries suffer slow executions despite query optimization, DBAs typically invoke automated query rewriting tools to recommend ``lean'' equivalents that are conducive to faster execution. The rewritings are usually achieved via transformation rules, but these rules are limited in scope and difficult to update in a production system. Recently, LLM-based techniques have also been suggested, but they are prone to semantic and syntactic errors.
We investigate here how the remarkable cognitive capabilities of LLMs can be leveraged for performant query rewriting while incorporating safeguards and optimizations to ensure correctness and efficiency. Our study shows that these goals can be progressively achieved through incorporation of (a) an ensemble suite of basic prompts, (b) database-sensitive prompts via redundancy removal and selectivity-based rewriting rules, and (c) LLM token probability-guided rewrite paths. Further, a suite of logic-based and statistical tools can be used to check for semantic violations in the rewrites prior to DBA consideration.
We have implemented the above LLM-infused techniques in the LITHE system, and evaluated complex analytic queries from standard benchmarks on contemporary database platforms. The results show significant performance improvements for slow queries, over both SOTA rewriters and the native optimizer. For instance, with TPC-DS on PostgreSQL, the GM of runtime speedups was a high 13.2 over the native optimizer, whereas SOTA only gave 4.9.
Overall, LITHE is a promising step toward viable LLM-based advisory tools for ameliorating enterprise query performance.

keyInsights: LLMs can discover significant optimization opportunities by transforming SQL queries in 'query space,' capabilities often beyond the scope of traditional, plan-space-focused database optimizers.

Effective integration of LLMs in database optimization requires a combination of sophisticated prompt engineering, the infusion of database-specific context (schema, statistics), and robust multi-stage verification mechanisms.

The MCTS-guided search based on token probabilities helps navigate the LLM's generative process, improving rewrite quality and reliability while mitigating the risk of incorrect or non-performant outputs.
originalProblem: Database query optimizers frequently struggle with complex, machine-generated, or poorly structured SQL queries, resulting in suboptimal execution plans and significant performance degradation.

Traditional rule-based SQL-to-SQL rewriting tools are limited by their fixed rule sets, which restricts their ability to discover diverse and novel optimizations for a wide range of real-world queries.
Early LLM-based query rewriting methods often suffer from 'hallucinations,' producing semantically or syntactically incorrect SQL, or suggesting rewrites that do not actually improve performance.
results: On the TPC-DS benchmark, LITHE achieved a 13.2x geometric mean speedup in execution time over native optimizers and produced 26 cost-productive rewrites (greater than 1.5x speedup), significantly outperforming state-of-the-art rewriters with a 4.9x speedup.

The system demonstrated strong generalizability, yielding positive results on datasets unknown to GPT-4o and maintaining good performance even when using smaller, quantized LLMs like LLaMA 3.1 70B (achieving a 10.9x time speedup).

LITHE successfully identified and prevented runtime regressions among its recommended rewrites and provided accurate, LLM-generated reasoning for performance improvements at an average LLM inference cost of only a few cents per query.
solution: LITHE implements a five-stage pipeline that combines LLM-based query rewriting with robust verification steps including syntax checking, estimated cost analysis, statistical/logic-based semantic equivalence tests, and runtime regression identification.

The system uses an ensemble of 'database-sensitive' prompts, enriched with logical schema and statistical selectivities, to guide LLMs in generating intelligent, performance-beneficial rewrites directly in the query space.

A Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) approach, leveraging LLM token probabilities, is employed to intelligently explore the rewrite search space and mitigate LLM 'hallucinations' when the model expresses low confidence.
paper_summary.summary: The LITHE system, developed by researchers at the Indian Institute of Science and Microsoft Research India, utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs) to transform inefficient SQL queries into semantically equivalent and more performant alternatives. This approach achieved a 13.2x geometric mean speedup in query execution time over native optimizers and generated 26 cost-productive rewrites on TPC-DS, outperforming state-of-the-art rewriters while ensuring correctness.

title: Query Rewriting via LLMs

abstract: Training effective Text-to-SQL models remains challenging due to the scarcity of high-quality, diverse, and structurally complex datasets. Existing methods either rely on limited human-annotated corpora, or synthesize datasets directly by simply prompting LLMs without explicit control over SQL structures, often resulting in limited structural diversity and complexity. To address this, we introduce EvolSQL, a structure-aware data synthesis framework that evolves SQL queries from seed data into richer and more semantically diverse forms. EvolSQL starts with an exploratory Query-SQL expansion to broaden question diversity and improve schema coverage, and then applies an adaptive directional evolution strategy using six atomic transformation operators derived from the SQL Abstract Syntax Tree to progressively increase query complexity across relational, predicate, aggregation, and nesting dimensions. An execution-grounded SQL refinement module and schema-aware deduplication further ensure the creation of high-quality, structurally diverse mapping pairs. Experimental results show that a 7B model fine-tuned on our data outperforms one trained on the much larger SynSQL dataset using only 1/18 of the data.

keyInsights: Systematic evolution of SQL queries through atomic structural transformations can generate highly complex and information-dense training data.
An adaptive evolution strategy, combining feasibility scores and scarcity weights, is essential for balanced structural coverage and valid query generation.
Integrating execution-grounded refinement and explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning paths significantly enhances the quality of synthetic data and improves model generalization.
originalProblem: Scarcity of high-quality, diverse, and structurally complex human-annotated Text-to-SQL training data.
Existing LLM-based data synthesis methods lack explicit control over SQL structural complexity, leading to limited diversity.
Massive-scale data synthesis often suffers from low data efficiency, requiring extensive data volumes for competitive performance.
results: EVOLSQL-Qwen-7B achieved 65.1% execution accuracy on the BIRD development set, outperforming OmniSQL-7B (63.9%) while utilizing approximately 1/18 of its training data volume.
The synthetic dataset exhibits substantially higher structural complexity (e.g., 130% increase in JOINs, increased CTEs) and semantic diversity compared to existing benchmarks.
Demonstrated consistent performance improvements across all SQL difficulty levels on BIRD and an average of 6.1% improvement on five cross-domain generalization benchmarks.
solution: An Exploratory Query-SQL Expansion (EQE) stage broadens semantic scope and schema coverage, generating novel NL-SQL pairs with execution-grounded refinement.
Operator-Guided SQL Evolution (OGE) systematically increases reasoning complexity using six atomic transformation operators and an adaptive directional strategy.
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) solution synthesis, followed by schema-aware deduplication, ensures high data quality and reduces semantic redundancy.
paper_summary.summary: EVOLSQL, developed by researchers from Beihang University and Peking University, introduces a structure-aware evolutionary framework for synthesizing high-quality Text-to-SQL data. It achieves an execution accuracy of 65.1% on the BIRD development set, surpassing prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning methods with significantly less training data and demonstrating robust cross-domain generalization.

title: EvolSQL: Structure-Aware Evolution for Scalable Text-to-SQL Data Synthesis

abstract: Natural Language to SQL (NL2SQL) has seen significant advancements with large language models (LLMs). However, these models often depend on closed-source systems and high computational resources, posing challenges in data privacy and deployment. In contrast, small language models (SLMs) struggle with NL2SQL tasks, exhibiting poor performance and incompatibility with existing frameworks. To address these issues, we introduce Feather-SQL, a new lightweight framework tailored for SLMs. Feather-SQL improves SQL executability and accuracy through 1) schema pruning and linking, 2) multi-path and multi-candidate generation. Additionally, we introduce the 1+1 Model Collaboration Paradigm, which pairs a strong general-purpose chat model with a fine-tuned SQL specialist, combining strong analytical reasoning with high-precision SQL generation. Experimental results on BIRD demonstrate that Feather-SQL improves NL2SQL performance on SLMs, with around 10% boost for models without fine-tuning. The proposed paradigm raises the accuracy ceiling of SLMs to 54.76%, highlighting its effectiveness.
title: Feather-SQL: A Lightweight NL2SQL Framework with Dual-Model Collaboration Paradigm for Small Language Models

abstract: Text-to-SQL has recently achieved impressive progress, yet remains difficult to apply effectively in real-world scenarios. This gap stems from the reliance on single static workflows, fundamentally limiting scalability to out-of-distribution and long-tail scenarios. Instead of requiring users to select suitable methods through extensive experimentation, we attempt to enable systems to adaptively construct workflows at inference time. Through theoretical and empirical analysis, we demonstrate that optimal dynamic policies consistently outperform the best static workflow, with performance gains fundamentally driven by heterogeneity across candidate workflows. Motivated by this, we propose SquRL, a reinforcement learning framework that enhances LLMs' reasoning capability in adaptive workflow construction. We design a rule-based reward function and introduce two effective training mechanisms: dynamic actor masking to encourage broader exploration, and pseudo rewards to improve training efficiency. Experiments on widely-used Text-to-SQL benchmarks demonstrate that dynamic workflow construction consistently outperforms the best static workflow methods, with especially pronounced gains on complex and out-of-distribution queries. The codes are available at this https URL

keyInsights: The optimal dynamic policy consistently performs no worse than the best static workflow, with performance gains driven by the complementary strengths and heterogeneity of different candidate workflows.
Reinforcement learning can effectively enable LLMs to orchestrate specialized modular components ('actors') for multi-step reasoning tasks, separating strategy selection from content generation.

Hybrid reward mechanisms, combining a detailed rule-based reward function with carefully integrated pseudo rewards, are crucial for efficient RL training in environments with sparse execution feedback.
originalProblem: Existing Text-to-SQL systems often rely on single, static workflows, which struggle to generalize to diverse and complex real-world query distributions.
Static pipelines are ineffective for out-of-distribution (OOD) and long-tail queries, limiting the practical applicability and scalability of Text-to-SQL solutions.
The necessity for extensive, manual experimentation to select or design suitable workflows for varied contexts is inefficient and impractical.
results: SquRL consistently outperformed all static baselines on Text-to-SQL benchmarks, including Spider, BIRD, and SynSQL, with SquRL-7B achieving 86.58% on Spider-Test and 67.60% on Bird-Dev.
The framework demonstrated a +7.97% improvement over the best static baseline on SynSQL's 'Highly Complex' subset, showing particular gains on complex and out-of-distribution queries.

Ablation studies confirmed that both Supervised Fine-Tuning for structural learning and Reinforcement Learning for refinement are critical, and dynamic actor masking and limited pseudo rewards enhanced robustness and training efficiency.
solution: The SquRL (Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Workflows) framework allows Large Language Models (LLMs) to autonomously construct task-specific workflows for Text-to-SQL queries at inference time.

The approach involves a two-stage process: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) teaches the policy model to generate valid workflow structures, followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL) to refine workflow selection using execution feedback.
Introduced techniques like a rule-based reward function, dynamic actor masking, and pseudo rewards to address challenges of sparse feedback and exploration in RL for Text-to-SQL.
paper_summary.summary: An adaptive reinforcement learning framework, SquRL, enables large language models to dynamically construct optimal execution workflows for Text-to-SQL tasks. It consistently outperforms static pipeline methods across various benchmarks, particularly for complex and out-of-distribution queries, by enhancing generalization through adaptive workflow selection.

title: Beyond Static Pipelines: Learning Dynamic Workflows for Text-to-SQL

abstract: SQL query rewriting aims to reformulate a query into a more efficient form while preserving equivalence. Most existing methods rely on predefined rewrite rules. However, such rule-based approaches face fundamental limitations: (1) fixed rule sets generalize poorly to novel query patterns and struggle with complex queries; (2) a wide range of effective rewriting strategies cannot be fully captured by declarative rules. To overcome these issues, we propose using large language models (LLMs) to generate rewrites. LLMs can capture complex strategies, such as evaluation reordering and CTE rewriting. Despite this potential, directly applying LLMs often results in performance regressions or non-equivalent rewrites due to a lack of execution awareness and semantic grounding. To address these challenges, We present E3-Rewrite, an LLM-based SQL rewriting framework that produces executable, equivalent, and efficient queries. It integrates two core components: a context construction module and a reinforcement learning framework. First, the context module leverages execution plans and retrieved demonstrations to build bottleneck-aware prompts that guide inference-time rewriting. Second, we design a reward function targeting executability, equivalence, and efficiency, evaluated via syntax checks, equivalence verification, and cost estimation. Third, to ensure stable multi-objective learning, we adopt a staged curriculum that first emphasizes executability and equivalence, then gradually incorporates efficiency. Across multiple SQL benchmarks, our experiments demonstrate that E3-Rewrite can shorten query execution time by as much as 25.6% relative to leading baselines, while also producing up to 24.4% more rewrites that meet strict equivalence criteria. These gains extend to challenging query patterns that prior approaches could not effectively optimize.

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

title: E3-Rewrite: Learning to Rewrite SQL for Executability, Equivalence,and Efficiency

abstract: The advancement of Text-to-SQL systems is currently hindered by the scarcity of high-quality training data and the limited reasoning capabilities of models in complex scenarios. In this paper, we propose a holistic framework that addresses these issues through a dual-centric approach. From a Data-Centric perspective, we construct an iterative data factory that synthesizes RL-ready data characterized by high correctness and precise semantic-logic alignment, ensured by strict verification. From a Model-Centric perspective, we introduce a novel Agentic Reinforcement Learning framework. This framework employs a Diversity-Aware Cold Start stage to initialize a robust policy, followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to refine the agent's reasoning via environmental feedback. Extensive experiments on BIRD and Spider benchmarks demonstrate that our synergistic approach achieves state-of-the-art performance among single-model methods.

keyInsights: Synthesizing high-fidelity, execution-verified training data, free from 'logic noise,' is critical for stable and effective Reinforcement Learning in complex Text-to-SQL environments.

Combining a 'Diversity-Aware Cold Start' with 'Group Relative Policy Optimization' in an agentic RL framework can stabilize policy refinement and significantly enhance multi-step reasoning and self-correction, overcoming sparse reward challenges.

A synergistic approach that simultaneously improves both data quality and model optimization yields superior Text-to-SQL performance, particularly for complex queries requiring robust logical inference.
originalProblem: Text-to-SQL models suffer from data scarcity, where high-quality, complex, and 'RL-ready' training data is expensive to annotate and prone to 'logic noise' in synthetic generation.

Current models exhibit limited reasoning and self-correction capabilities, as Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) has a 'loss-evaluation mismatch' and traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL) faces sparse rewards, instability, and inefficient exploration.
Challenging benchmarks like BIRD require robust multi-step reasoning and precise semantic-logic alignment which existing methods struggle to provide consistently.
results: Achieved an Execution Accuracy of 70.66% on the BIRD benchmark, improving to 72.10% with self-consistency, setting a new state-of-the-art for single-model Text-to-SQL systems.
Obtained an Execution Accuracy of 89.13% on the Spider benchmark, demonstrating competitive performance even against multi-agent frameworks.

Outperformed various strong baselines, including zero-shot LLMs (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro-preview at 67.52% on BIRD) and other open-source agentic/RL models, validating the effectiveness of the integrated framework.
solution: Developed an 'RL-ready' iterative data pipeline with structural-aware synthesis (DAG-based database augmentation, SQL decomposition) and a K-cycle iterative Gen-as-Check refinement loop, using execution verification and an LLM-as-a-judge for 'zero-noise' gold labels.

Introduced a two-stage Agentic Reinforcement Learning framework, comprising a Diversity-Aware Cold Start Supervised Fine-Tuning for robust interactive capabilities and Agentic RL with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for stable learning in sparse-reward environments.

Incorporated an inference phase with a context augmentation pipeline (database profiling, semantic description, dynamic pruning) and multi-turn refinement using execution feedback to iteratively correct SQL queries.
paper_summary.summary: The AGRO-SQL framework integrates a high-fidelity data synthesis pipeline with an agentic reinforcement learning approach for Text-to-SQL tasks. It establishes new state-of-the-art performance among single-model systems, achieving 72.10% Execution Accuracy on the BIRD benchmark and 89.13% on the Spider benchmark.

title: AGRO-SQL: Agentic Group-Relative Optimization with High-Fidelity Data Synthesis

abstract: SQL queries in real world analytical environments, whether written by humans or generated automatically often suffer from syntax errors, inefficiency, or semantic misalignment, especially in complex OLAP scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose SQLGovernor, an LLM powered SQL toolkit that unifies multiple functionalities, including syntax correction, query rewriting, query modification, and consistency verification within a structured framework enhanced by knowledge management. SQLGovernor introduces a fragment wise processing strategy to enable fine grained rewriting and localized error correction, significantly reducing the cognitive load on the LLM. It further incorporates a hybrid self learning mechanism guided by expert feedback, allowing the system to continuously improve through DBMS output analysis and rule validation. Experiments on benchmarks such as BIRD and BIRD CRITIC, as well as industrial datasets, show that SQLGovernor consistently boosts the performance of base models by up to 10%, while minimizing reliance on manual expertise. Deployed in production environments, SQLGovernor demonstrates strong practical utility and effective performance.
title: SQLGovernor: An LLM-powered SQL Toolkit for Real World Application

abstract: While large language models (LLMs) have substantially improved Text-to-SQL generation, a pronounced gap remains between AI systems and human experts on challenging benchmarks such as BIRD-SQL. We argue this gap stems largely from the prevailing single-pass paradigm, which lacks the iterative reasoning, schema exploration, and error-correction behaviors that humans naturally employ. To address this limitation, we introduce SQL-Trail, a multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) agentic framework for Text-to-SQL. Rather than producing a query in one shot, SQL-Trail interacts with the database environment and uses execution feedback to iteratively refine its predictions. Our approach centers on two key ideas: (i) an adaptive turn-budget allocation mechanism that scales the agent's interaction depth to match question difficulty, and (ii) a composite reward panel that jointly incentivizes SQL correctness and efficient exploration. Across benchmarks, SQL-Trail sets a new state of the art and delivers strong data efficiency--up to 18x higher than prior single-pass RL state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our 7B and 14B models outperform substantially larger proprietary systems by 5% on average, underscoring the effectiveness of interactive, agentic workflows for robust Text-to-SQL generation.

keyInsights: Emulating human-like iterative reasoning and leveraging interleaved execution feedback through multi-turn RL significantly improves Text-to-SQL performance and generalization beyond single-pass methods.

A carefully designed composite reward panel, particularly the bi-gram similarity reward, offers dense, token-level learning signals that stabilize training and guide the agent towards correct SQL structures.

Supervised Fine-Tuning with distilled trajectories is crucial for bootstrapping smaller open-source models, enabling them to effectively utilize complex multi-turn agentic workflows and providing a strong foundation for subsequent RL optimization.
originalProblem: A significant performance gap exists between AI systems and human experts in complex Text-to-SQL tasks due to the limitations of traditional single-pass query generation.
Current models struggle with iterative reasoning, dynamic schema exploration, and self-correction based on execution feedback, which are crucial for human proficiency.
Challenges include ambiguous natural language, complex database schemas, strict SQL syntax, and the high computational cost and data scarcity for training advanced Text-to-SQL models.
results: SQL-TRAIL achieved state-of-the-art accuracy, with its 7B model reaching 64.2% Execution Accuracy on the BIRD-dev benchmark, outperforming larger proprietary models (e.g., Sonnet-3.7) and showing a 7-18x gain in data efficiency over same-scale baselines.

The multi-turn RL approach demonstrated strong out-of-distribution generalization, with SQL-TRAIL-7B effectively transferring to the BIRD-dev domain without direct training on it, and showing enhanced robustness on Spider-DK, Spider-Syn, and Spider-Realistic benchmarks.

Analysis confirmed that multi-turn RL consistently outperforms single-pass RL, showing superior OOD generalization (+3 percentage points on BIRD-dev) and continued performance scaling, while also achieving higher reasoning efficiency by adaptively allocating turns and tokens.
solution: Developed SQL-TRAIL, an LLM-based agent that employs a ReAct-style multi-turn reinforcement learning framework to interact with a SQL execution engine.
Utilized a two-phase training pipeline, starting with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) based on distilled trajectories from a proprietary teacher model, followed by Multi-turn Reinforcement Learning.

Implemented an adaptive turn-budget allocation mechanism and a composite reward panel with six terms (execution, turns, syntax, n-gram, schema, format) to provide dense, step-wise guidance during RL training.
paper_summary.summary: SQL-TRAIL introduces a multi-turn reinforcement learning agent for Text-to-SQL, enabling iterative interaction with a SQL execution environment to refine queries. This approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the BIRD-dev benchmark with a 7B model reaching 64.2% Execution Accuracy, while demonstrating an order-of-magnitude improvement in data efficiency over previous methods.

title: SQL-Trail: Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning with Interleaved Feedback for Text-to-SQL

abstract:
In Text-to-SQL, execution feedback is essential for guiding large language
models (LLMs) to reason accurately and generate reliable SQL queries. However,
existing methods treat execution feedback solely as a post-hoc signal for
correction or selection, failing to integrate it into the generation process.
This limitation hinders their ability to address reasoning errors as they
occur, ultimately reducing query accuracy and robustness. To address this
issue, we propose ReEx-SQL (Reasoning with Execution-Aware Reinforcement
Learning), a framework for Text-to-SQL that enables models to interact with the
database during decoding and dynamically adjust their reasoning based on
execution feedback. ReEx-SQL introduces an execution-aware reasoning paradigm
that interleaves intermediate SQL execution into reasoning paths, facilitating
context-sensitive revisions. It achieves this through structured prompts with
markup tags and a stepwise rollout strategy that integrates execution feedback
into each stage of generation. To supervise policy learning, we develop a
composite reward function that includes an exploration reward, explicitly
encouraging effective database interaction. Additionally, ReEx-SQL adopts a
tree-based decoding strategy to support exploratory reasoning, enabling dynamic
expansion of alternative reasoning paths. Notably, ReEx-SQL achieves 88.8% on
Spider and 64.9% on BIRD at the 7B scale, surpassing the standard reasoning
baseline by 2.7% and 2.6%, respectively. It also shows robustness, achieving
85.2% on Spider-Realistic with leading performance. In addition, its
tree-structured decoding improves efficiency and performance over linear
decoding, reducing inference time by 51.9% on the BIRD development set.

organization_info.name: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ)

keyInsights: Integrating real-time execution feedback directly into the LLM's reasoning process significantly reduces syntactic and semantic errors by enabling dynamic self-correction.

A comprehensive, execution-aware composite reward function, particularly the execution reward, is critical for effectively guiding reinforcement learning to produce correct and semantically aligned SQL.

Tree-structured decoding enhances both the accuracy and efficiency of SQL generation by allowing for adaptive exploration of reasoning paths, reducing inference time by over 50% compared to linear decoding.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based Text-to-SQL methods rely on static reasoning, leading to error accumulation and an inability to verify or revise intermediate decisions during generation.
Lack of real-time database interaction results in LLMs frequently generating syntactically incorrect SQL queries and semantic misalignments with user intent.
Prior solutions often decouple verification from generation (post-hoc correction), risking the alteration of correct predictions or the introduction of new errors without integrated reasoning.
